CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally organized into background → model → experiments → related work, and key notation (e.g., G_i,j, {S,O,X,−}^k) is introduced, but the document suffers from heavy duplication, injected LaTeX/artifact noise and repeated paragraphs (e.g., multiple near-identical blocks beginning “Among these models…” and repeated citations), which disrupts logical flow and makes early statement of contributions inconsistent. Figures/tables are referenced (Figure 2, Tables 3–4) but their absence in the provided text and several broken sentences/phrasing (e.g., “Compared to discrimination, gain in insertion is less verbose”) reduce clarity. Overall the notation is mostly defined, but presentation and proofreading issues (repetition, formatting artifacts, occasional grammatical oddities) mean the paper is adequate but some sections are hard to follow.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP problem (local discourse coherence) and proposes a neural extension of the well-known entity-grid model (Grid-CNN / Extended Grid-CNN). It evaluates on standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, DUC’03 summary rating) and corpora (WSJ, DUC) and compares to relevant prior work (Barzilay & Lapata, Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube, Li & Hovy), using expected learning/evaluation protocols (pairwise ranking, CNNs, dropout/early stopping). The topic, methods, datasets, and comparisons make it a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a well-recognized problem (automatic local coherence modeling) by neuralizing the popular entity-grid representation with a convolutional model, explicitly tackling the curse of dimensionality in discrete grids. It reports consistent empirical gains (e.g., ~4% in discrimination and ~2.5% in insertion) and state-of-the-art results across standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating), so practitioners studying coherence or sentence ordering will likely adopt or extend it. However, the contribution is an incremental methodological advance (applying CNNs and distributed representations to an existing representation) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable novel angle by neuralizing the established entity-grid approach—introducing distributed representations and a convolutional architecture that operates over entity grids to capture long-range entity transitions (sections 3 and 6). It explicitly distinguishes itself from prior neural work (Li & Hovy 2014) by operating on entity grids rather than sentence-level RNN/recursive models, and it integrates entity-specific features into the neural model (Section 3.1). While the problem (coherence modeling) is well-studied and the contribution builds on existing concepts (Barzilay & Lapata 2008; Elsner & Charniak 2011), the methodological shift to convnets over grids and end-to-end pairwise ranking is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated extension that would be of interest to experts.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on standard coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion) using WSJ and on DUC’03 summary coherence, and compares to strong baselines including entity-grid variants and a graph-based model, which supports their core claims. However, there are no systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of individual entity-specific features, filter/window sizes, or training on insertion), and only a brief discussion of poorer insertion gains and the excluded negative Li & Hovy run. Related work coverage is broad and fair, and some implementation choices (optimizers, dropout, toolkits/command-line flags for baselines) are given, but full reproducibility is limited by missing architecture/hyperparameter specifics and code. Overall the experiments are adequate but missing important analyses and ablations.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and strong baselines (random, Guinaudeau & Strube graph model, Barzilay & Lapata grid variants and Elsner & Charniak’s extended grid) and evaluates on standard tasks/benchmarks (WSJ discrimination/insertion and DUC’03 summary rating), using existing toolkits (Brown coherence, cohere6) to ensure comparable conditions (see §5.1 and dataset choice discussion). The authors acknowledge limitations (they excluded the Li & Hovy (2014) distributed-sentence model after poor replication and explicitly note that Extended Grid-CNN uses only three of the nine Elsner & Charniak features). Missing are direct experimental comparisons to some other recent/related approaches (e.g., Feng et al. 2014 / Lin et al. 2011 RST/discourse-based models) and the feature-subset difference makes the Extended Grid vs Extended Grid-CNN comparison less clean, so comparisons are good overall but with minor omissions and one fairness caveat.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are empirically supported: it compares Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN to appropriate baselines (Random, graph-based, grid and extended-grid) using standard tasks/datasets (WSJ sentence-ordering tasks and DUC'03 summary ratings) and reports absolute improvements (see Sec. 5.1, 5.3 and 4.2). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check — the model/learning approach is described (pairwise ranking, wide convolution) but not formalized with proofs, so nothing to verify there. The experimental methodology is mostly sound (use of established toolkits/implementations, dev set, early stopping), but the paper omits statistical significance testing, full numeric tables/hyperparameter details and exact WSJ train/test sizes, which weakens confidence in the robustness of the reported gains. Given reported improvements and reasonable baselines, the conclusions are plausible but would be stronger with significance tests and more complete experimental detail.
